rows.sat_rat: Rejected/resolved units are a known hotspot where physical markings and event records still diverge. Resolving physical-vs-electronic conflicts on resolved units is manual and slow. Requires careful investigator judgement to reconcile markings and events. Unresolved conflicts risk compliance findings and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restored serials carrying the wrong lifecycle status
rows.plain: Make sure restored product identification numbers show the correct current status. This prevents products from being handled as if they are in the wrong stage.
rows.imp_rat: Restored serials carrying the wrong lifecycle status misrepresents serial state after recovery; severe because incorrect statuses can wrongly enable or block units.
rows.sat_rat: Restore paths after interruption are less well controlled, so wrong-status restoration recurs. Restore operations complete quickly when invoked. Restoring correct lifecycle status after recovery needs skilled intervention. Wrong restored status causes reconciliation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of labels printed with unreadable or incomplete codes after restart
rows.plain: Reduce the number of labels that have missing or unreadable codes after work starts again. This helps people identify and track the products correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Labels printed unreadable/incomplete after restart produces defective codes at a vulnerable moment; severe because restart defects can escape if verification is disrupted.
rows.sat_rat: Restart often produces a burst of poor prints that verification catches but does not prevent. Post-restart reverification adds line time. Restart print-quality checks depend on operator attention. Unreadable post-restart labels waste materials and serials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers with conflicting statuses across line and track-and-trace records
rows.plain: Make sure each product identification number has the same status in the production records and the tracking records. This prevents conflicting information about a product.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting statuses across line and track-and-trace records creates reconciliation ambiguity; severe because it can allow wrongly-statused units to release.
rows.sat_rat: Line-to-T&T status conflicts remain common due to timing and integration gaps between systems. Cross-system reconciliation between line and T&T adds closure time. Resolving status conflicts needs cross-system knowledge. Conflicts delay reporting to regulators and hold product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required printed identifier elements omitted from verification scope
rows.plain: Make sure every required product identification detail is included in the checking process. This helps catch missing or incorrect information.
rows.imp_rat: Required identifier elements omitted from verification scope means the verify check is incomplete; severe because unchecked fields can carry defects to market.
rows.sat_rat: Verification recipes typically cover core identifier elements, but supplementary printed elements are inconsistently included in scope, leaving recurring gaps. Verification scope is defined at setup and does not add run time once set. Defining which printed elements to verify requires QA judgement that is easy to under-specify. An omitted element from scope is cheap operationally but risks costly downstream rejections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of finished-unit serial identifiers missing from the completed run record
rows.plain: Make sure the record of a completed production run includes the identifying number for every finished item. This makes it possible to trace each item back to that run.
rows.imp_rat: Finished-unit serials missing from the completed run record leaves gaps in the traceability record; severe because incomplete records impede release and audit.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation reports catch most missing serials, but late or dropped events still leave occasional gaps at closure. Completeness is checked at closure, adding reconciliation time. Reconciling missing serials needs analyst attention. Missing records delay batch release, tying up inventory value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applied codes that cannot be decoded during verification
rows.plain: Make sure the codes placed on products can be read and understood during checking. This helps confirm that the right information is attached to each product.
rows.imp_rat: Applied codes that cannot be decoded during verification undermine the verify step; severe because undecodable codes cannot be authenticated in the field.
rows.sat_rat: Verification readers reliably decode most codes when well set up, but marginal print quality causes recurring decode failures. Decoding happens inline in real time. Camera and lighting setup to ensure decodability demands technician skill. Undecodable codes waste labeled units and serials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of label fields mapped to the wrong encoded or human-readable value
rows.plain: Make sure each label field shows the correct value, both in the printed text and in the machine-readable code. This prevents labels from giving people or systems the wrong information.
rows.imp_rat: Label fields mapped to the wrong encoded/human-readable value corrupts printed identity data; severe because mislabeled fields can misroute or misidentify product.
rows.sat_rat: Validated label templates catch most mapping errors, but new-template errors still surface before qualification. Field mapping is a template-design activity, not a runtime delay. Correct field-to-value mapping requires skilled template configuration and review. Mismapped fields can invalidate an entire label design run at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of assigned serial identifiers with unresolved disposition at the product-release decision
rows.plain: Reduce the number of product identification numbers whose final status is still unclear when deciding whether a batch can be released. This helps ensure only correctly accounted-for products are approved.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved disposition at release leaves serial state ambiguous at the decision gate; severe because releasing with undetermined status risks shipping non-compliant units.
rows.sat_rat: Systems report status but reliably driving every assigned identifier to a defined disposition by release is an inconsistent, gap-prone practice. Resolving open dispositions before the release decision extends release timelines. Determining correct disposition for stranded identifiers requires experienced serialization/QA judgement. Chasing unresolved dispositions ties up review labor per lot.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of incomplete traceability coverage while a failed component is repaired or replaced
rows.plain: Keep the period as short as possible when equipment records cannot fully show what happened while a failed part is repaired or replaced. This helps maintain a clear history of the process.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete coverage during a component repair is a temporary availability issue rather than a core capture defect (relevance 6). Prolonged gaps still create untraceable production windows requiring conservative disposition (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Manual bridging procedures during a repair are inconsistently applied, so the certainty of full coverage during the window is low. Repairs still require sourcing spare parts, dispatching technicians, and revalidating, so coverage gaps commonly stretch over hours or a full shift despite redundancy attempts. Restoring traceability coverage quickly demands scarce technicians who understand both the mechanical component and the traceability software linkage. Lost or unverifiable production during the gap forces holds, rework, or quarantine that carry real material and throughput cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of associating a recovered identifier with the wrong package record
rows.plain: Make sure a recovered identification number is matched to the correct package record. A wrong match could make the package history inaccurate.
rows.imp_rat: Associating a recovered identifier with the wrong package record is an edge-case recovery scenario, less central to routine operation (relevance 6). When it occurs it silently corrupts a specific record with recall-relevant consequences (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recovery re-association is largely manual and unverified, so wrong-record linking is a persistent, poorly-mitigated risk. Manual re-association of recovered codes is a slow, off-line task after a stoppage. Matching a recovered identifier to the correct record relies on operator judgement with limited system support. A wrong re-association can require costly rework or invalidate a batch's genealogy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unauthorized changes to approved serialization master data before or during a production run
rows.plain: Have fewer unapproved changes to the item-numbering information used for production, either before or during a production run. This helps ensure items are identified correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized changes to serialization master data compromise data integrity governing assignment; severe because tampered master data can corrupt a whole run and breach data-integrity rules.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access and change control are mature, though mid-run master-data locking is not universally airtight. Change controls add procedural time but not runtime delay. Enforcing master-data change control depends on disciplined role-based access. Unauthorized master-data changes can spoil runs at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of status events applied to a serial other than the intended serial
rows.plain: Apply a status update to the correct item number every time, rather than to a different item. This prevents records from being attached to the wrong item.
rows.imp_rat: Status events applied to the wrong serial corrupt lifecycle state central to track-and-trace; severe because incorrect statuses can wrongly enable or block units.
rows.sat_rat: Event-to-serial routing is systematized but manual status corrections still occasionally hit the wrong serial. Status events post in near-real-time. Automated event routing reduces manual matching effort. Misapplied status events cause reconciliation labor rather than material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a partially obscured identifier as readable
rows.plain: Avoid treating an identifier as readable when part of it is hidden or blocked. This prevents the wrong item from being recorded or handled.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a partially obscured identifier as readable is a verify leniency error; moderately severe because degraded codes may fail later downstream scans.
rows.sat_rat: Verification cameras sometimes accept partially decodable codes, so partial obscuration slipping through remains a real gap. Grading at speed adds no operator time but marginal reads can stall decisions. Setting grade thresholds to reject partial obscuration needs vision-grading knowledge. Accepting an obscured code that later fails causes downstream rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of configured date or lot formats that violate destination-market rules
rows.plain: Set up fewer date or batch-number formats that break the rules of the countries where the goods will be sent. This helps ensure the labels meet local requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Date/lot formats violating market rules make labels non-compliant; severe because the destination may reject shipments over format non-conformance.
rows.sat_rat: Market-rule templates exist but keeping them current across many jurisdictions leaves recurring compliance gaps. Format configuration is done once per market at setup. Knowing each market's date/lot rules requires specialized regulatory knowledge. Non-compliant formats can trigger rejected shipments at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrect batch, lot, or product values are loaded for the scheduled order
rows.plain: Load the correct production, lot, and product information for the scheduled order. Wrong settings could cause the wrong product or batch to be made.
rows.imp_rat: Loading incorrect batch/lot/product values for the scheduled order mislabels an entire run at the source, feeding into all records (relevance 7). A wrong lot value can trigger mass mislabeling with recall and regulatory impact (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: ERP/MES-driven order download with scan-to-confirm and formatted labels reliably loads correct values in most modern lines, though manual overrides still allow occasional wrong-value loads. Order/recipe loading happens at changeover with negligible impact on run time. Operators still manually confirm batch/lot selections, leaving room for selection error under time pressure. Loading wrong lot values can spoil an entire run's labeling, a costly rework or scrap event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required serial-assignment, label-printing, or verification records are unavailable when the assessment begins
rows.plain: Make sure all required records about assigning serial numbers, printing labels, and checking them are available when the review starts. Missing records can make it impossible to judge whether the process followed the rules.
rows.imp_rat: Required records unavailable when assessment begins blocks verification of compliance; severe because missing records can force holds and fail audit.
rows.sat_rat: Records are generally retained and available, but fragmentation across line, site, and repository systems occasionally leaves records missing at assessment time. Retrieving records at assessment start can be slow when data is spread across systems. Knowing where and how to pull each record type requires system-specific expertise. Record retrieval effort adds preparation cost before each assessment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product SKUs assigned an incorrect printing or marking template
rows.plain: Make sure each product is assigned the correct printing or marking instructions, so packages show the right information.
rows.imp_rat: Assigning an incorrect printing/marking template to a SKU is a setup/configuration error less central to runtime capture (relevance 6). Wrong templates cause systematic mislabeling of an entire SKU run with compliance and customer fallout (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Template libraries and preview verification catch most errors, though manual SKU assignment still produces occasional mismatches. Assigning print/marking templates per SKU is a repetitive configuration task done during prep. Template-to-SKU mapping needs attentive configuration but is well within trained technician capability. A wrong template wastes materials and rework, keeping cost pressure moderate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of units reaching verification with a required code field missing
rows.plain: Make sure no unit reaches the checking step without all required code information. This helps ensure each unit can be identified and handled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Units reaching verification with a missing required code field means the print step failed; severe because incomplete identifiers cannot satisfy serialization requirements.
rows.sat_rat: Field-presence verification is standard and catches missing required codes consistently. Missing-field detection happens at verification without delay. Automated presence checks reduce reliance on operator inspection. Units failing for missing fields are rejected, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of commissioned serials lacking a corresponding labeled unit
rows.plain: Make sure every commissioned serial number has a matching labeled unit. This prevents products from having missing or mismatched identity information.
rows.imp_rat: Commissioned serials lacking a corresponding labeled unit creates phantom records; severe because it breaks reconciliation and can mask missing or diverted product.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation flags orphan serials reliably, though closing them out remains a manual burden. Detected at batch reconciliation rather than in real time. Chasing commissioned serials without units needs skilled reconciliation. Investigation labor is the main cost of these discrepancies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers lacking a valid link to the applicable release lot
rows.plain: Make sure every serial number is correctly connected to the release batch it belongs to. This supports accurate product tracking and trustworthy records.
rows.imp_rat: Serials lacking a valid link to the release lot breaks lot traceability; severe because unlinked serials cannot be reconciled at release or during recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Commissioning normally binds identifiers to a lot, but reprints, exceptions, and manual interventions still produce identifiers with weak or missing lot links. Lot-linking is applied at commissioning so timing is not the concern. Association is automated, needing little operator skill when configured. Broken lot links trigger costly traceability investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable serialization obligations translated incorrectly into integration criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the applicable requirements for tracking individual products are translated correctly into instructions for connected systems. This helps those systems exchange and record the right information.
rows.imp_rat: Obligations translated incorrectly into integration criteria is an upstream design-quality gap; moderately severe as errors propagate but are caught before production if reviewed.
rows.sat_rat: Without standardized mappings, interpretation varies between people and mistranslations slip through unnoticed. Translating obligations into integration criteria is iterative and often revisited late, adding delay. Correct translation hinges on rare individuals who bridge regulatory language and technical architecture, making errors common. Misinterpretation drives expensive redesign but the interpretive step is cheap in materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serialization and aggregation messages failing to reach required upstream or downstream systems
rows.plain: Make sure the tracking messages needed by connected systems are delivered successfully. This prevents those systems from losing important information about products or packages.
rows.imp_rat: Serialization/aggregation messages failing to reach required systems undermines the end-to-end reporting that is core to traceability (relevance); missing regulatory reporting can cause compliance failures (severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Connectivity is testable at prep but heterogeneous partner systems make full message-delivery assurance a persistent gap. Configuring and verifying all upstream/downstream connections is slow, multi-party integration work. It requires cross-system integration expertise spanning line, site, and enterprise layers. Failed message delivery drives costly holds and reprocessing when discovered.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable serialization obligations lacking traceable evidence in the architecture evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the system review includes traceable proof for all applicable requirements about identifying and tracking products. This helps show that the system meets the required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Obligations lacking traceable evidence in the architecture evaluation is an audit-completeness gap; moderately severe because it weakens defensibility rather than producing direct defects.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices are inconsistently maintained, so many obligations lack durable evidence links. Assembling traceable evidence for each obligation is a laborious documentation task typically deferred. Building defensible traceability requires disciplined systems-engineering practice that many teams lack. Evidence gaps trigger audit findings and rework that cost far more than maintaining traces would.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serialization transactions lost or duplicated during enterprise-system transfer
rows.plain: Prevent tracking records from being lost or recorded twice when information moves between company computer systems. This keeps product records complete and accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Lost or duplicated serialization transactions during enterprise transfer directly corrupt the traceability chain (relevance), and duplicate/missing serial data can trigger recalls or regulatory rejection (severity). Capped at 6.5 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard messaging middleware provides delivery guarantees, but transfer loss and duplication remain recurring prep-validation findings. Setting up and testing enterprise-system transfer interfaces takes considerable integration time. Reliable EPCIS/message transfer configuration demands specialist middleware and integration skills. Lost or duplicated transactions cause reconciliation cost, partly contained by transaction logging.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required serialization entries missing at run closure
rows.plain: Make sure all required item-identification records are entered before the run is closed. This allows the items and their history to be tracked later.
rows.imp_rat: Required entries missing at run closure leaves incomplete records; severe because closure gaps block release and undermine audit defensibility.
rows.sat_rat: Closure checklists flag missing entries reliably but resolving them remains a manual, sometimes incomplete effort. Missing entries surface at run closure, extending closeout time. Resolving missing entries requires knowledgeable review. Incomplete records block release and hold inventory.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that serialization records are altered without authorized and traceable approval
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that product-tracking records are changed without permission or without a record showing who approved the change. This helps keep the records trustworthy and meet official requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Serialization records altered without traceable approval is a data-integrity breach; severe because unauthorized alterations can invalidate the entire audit trail and compliance standing.
rows.sat_rat: Validated, access-controlled serialization systems make unauthorized untraceable changes rare where GxP controls are enforced. Approval workflows for record changes add controlled delay but are routine. Validated systems handle authorization enforcement without special user skill. Access controls are already provisioned so marginal cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable serialization obligations omitted from the architecture evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the system design includes all rules for giving products unique identifying numbers and tracking them. This helps the product remain traceable and legally compliant.
rows.imp_rat: Obligations omitted from architecture evaluation is a design-completeness gap; moderately severe because missed requirements may surface later but are one step removed from production.
rows.sat_rat: Obligation checklists exist but are frequently incomplete for edge markets, so omissions are a recurring and consequential failure. Compiling the full obligation set across markets is a slow manual research effort that regulatory-intelligence teams rarely complete promptly. Identifying every applicable obligation demands scarce regulatory-affairs and serialization expertise that few evaluators possess in full. Missing an obligation surfaces later as costly rework or remediation, but the upfront research cost itself is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required serialization fields missing or invalid in the batch setup
rows.plain: Make sure the batch setup record contains all required information and that the information is valid. This prevents problems when equipment or materials are changed between batches.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/invalid serialization fields in batch setup misconfigures the run at the start; severe because setup errors can corrupt the entire batch's serialization.
rows.sat_rat: Setup validation checks catch most missing or invalid fields before the run starts. Pre-run verification adds setup time but prevents runtime loss. Operators must enter batch fields correctly, an error-prone manual step. Missing fields cause aborted setups and lost time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required serialization data connection is unavailable before run authorization
rows.plain: Make sure the required connection for recording each product's identity is working before the production run is approved. This prevents a run from starting without needed tracking information.
rows.imp_rat: A required data connection unavailable before run authorization delays or blocks proper setup; moderately severe as it prevents start but is caught before production.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-run connectivity checks are common but network, EPCIS, and provider outages still intermittently leave required connections unavailable before authorization. An unavailable data connection at authorization directly stalls run start, costing time. Diagnosing connectivity issues requires IT/integration skill not always on the floor. Stoppages waiting for connections are costly in idle line time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required regulatory or customer data elements omitted from configured code structures
rows.plain: Make sure all information required by authorities or customers is included in the product codes. This helps ensure the codes are complete and meet the required expectations.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required regulatory/customer data elements from code structures is a configuration concern rather than runtime capture, so moderately central (relevance 6). Missing mandated fields can trigger compliance rejections and customer penalties (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Well-established regulatory templates and checklists cover common markets, so omissions are uncommon though not eliminated. Mapping regulatory data elements into code structures is a defined but lengthy prep task. It requires regulatory-affairs expertise to know which elements each market mandates, a real skill dependency. Omitted mandatory elements risk rejected shipments, an expensive outcome that motivates careful review.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between parameter values used for identifier printing and verification
rows.plain: Make sure the settings used to print an identification label are the same as the settings used to check it. This prevents labels from being printed or accepted incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched printing vs verification parameters can cause valid codes to fail or bad codes to pass; severe because it undermines the reliability of the verify decision.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated systems sharing one recipe largely prevent print/verify mismatch, though loosely coupled setups still drift. Shared parameter sets avoid re-entry time between print and verify. Ensuring both stations use the same recipe needs integration setup skill. Parameter mismatch causes false rejects and wasted units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of provisioned serial records lost or corrupted before assignment
rows.plain: Fewer prepared tracking numbers should be lost or damaged before they are assigned to items. This helps every item receive the correct unique number.
rows.imp_rat: Provisioned records lost/corrupted before assignment threatens the source data for the job; severe because corrupted pools can produce invalid or duplicate assignments.
rows.sat_rat: Database persistence and backups make pre-assignment serial loss rare in validated systems. Loss is detected after the fact, adding recovery time. Recovering corrupted serial records needs specialized IT skill. Lost provisioned serials waste allocated ranges and investigation effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of retired serials returned to active status
rows.plain: Numbers that have been taken out of use should less often be put back into active use. This prevents two items from being given confusing or repeated identities.
rows.imp_rat: Retired serials returned to active status can reintroduce duplicate or invalid identifiers; severe because reactivated serials may authenticate as legitimate wrongly.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle state machines generally block retired-to-active transitions, keeping this rare. Status reactivation is a quick transaction when it occurs. Preventing retired-serial reactivation depends on status-model enforcement. Reactivated retired serials risk compliance problems more than direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unapproved serialization parameter changes being active at run start
rows.plain: At the start of a production run, settings for item tracking should be unlikely to have been changed without approval. This helps ensure the run begins with the correct approved setup.
rows.imp_rat: Unapproved parameter changes active at run start misconfigure serialization from the outset; severe because the entire run inherits the unapproved settings.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic change control and locked recipes make unapproved active changes uncommon in validated environments. Change control is a pre-run gate, not a runtime cost. Approval workflows offload the judgement from the operator to a controlled process. Unapproved changes can invalidate output at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of provisioned serials violating required format or allocation rules
rows.plain: Reduce the share of assigned unique identification numbers that use the wrong format or are assigned against the rules. This helps ensure each item can be tracked correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Provisioned serials violating format/allocation rules introduce non-compliant identifiers; severe because they can be rejected at reporting or market entry.
rows.sat_rat: Format/allocation-rule validation is a standard provisioning feature that catches most violations. Format validation runs instantly during provisioning. Rule configuration needs expertise but enforcement is automated thereafter. Format violations waste provisioned serials, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of doubting that every saleable unit has a valid unique identifier at certification
rows.plain: Be confident that every product being approved for sale has its own valid identifier. This makes each item easy to distinguish and properly track.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about unique validity at certification reflects assurance quality rather than a direct defect; moderately severe since low confidence delays certification but is not itself a shipped error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of traceability-system degradation occurring without an alert
rows.plain: Make sure a decline in the system that tracks product history is detected and reported, rather than going unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Traceability degradation occurring without an alert allows silent data loss (relevance) that may accumulate before detection (severity), but it is a monitoring-layer concern rather than a primary capture function.
rows.sat_rat: Most installed systems have limited self-diagnostics and weak alerting, so degradation frequently proceeds unannounced until it becomes failure. Silent degradation is often discovered only at periodic audits or batch reconciliation, so detection lag can span days. Recognizing subtle drift before an outright fault requires experienced eyes that most maintenance crews lack. Undetected degradation causes accumulated unverifiable product and costly retroactive investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexplained gaps in the serial identifier sequence during production
rows.plain: Avoid unexplained missing numbers in the sequence used to identify products during production. This helps keep every item traceable.
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained gaps in the serial sequence signal reconciliation problems; moderately severe as they trigger investigation but do not directly ship defects.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence tracking flags gaps reliably, but explaining them still requires manual reconciliation and some remain unresolved. Gap detection is automated post-run, not adding line time. Investigating gaps needs analyst skill to reconcile events. Unexplained gaps trigger investigation labor and potential batch holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serials provisioned without approved authorization
rows.plain: Reduce the number of unique identification numbers created without confirmed approval. This helps ensure every number is officially authorized and correctly assigned.
rows.imp_rat: Serials provisioned without approved authorization introduces unsanctioned numbers; severe because unauthorized serials may be rejected by regulators or fail authentication.
rows.sat_rat: Approval-gated provisioning is standard in validated systems, making unauthorized provisioning uncommon. Authorization checks are near-instant once workflows are configured. Electronic approval gating reduces reliance on operator judgement. Unauthorized provisioning is cheap to prevent with existing controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exhausting the provisioned serial pool before all batch units receive serials
rows.plain: Make sure there are enough unique product numbers for every unit in the group being processed. Running out could leave some units without the numbers needed to identify them.
rows.imp_rat: Exhausting the serial pool before all units are serialized stops assignment; moderately severe as it halts production but is generally caught before shipping unserialized units.
rows.sat_rat: Low-pool alerts exist, but under-provisioning still occasionally exhausts pools before batch completion. Pool depletion is monitored continuously without added delay. Planners must size pools correctly, needing some forecasting skill. Exhaustion mid-run halts the line, a costly stoppage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the configured serial allocation is exhausted before planned run output is completed
rows.plain: Make sure the supply of assigned identification numbers does not run out before the planned production is finished. This allows every item to receive the required unique number.
rows.imp_rat: Allocation exhausted before planned output halts assignment; moderately severe as it stops the run but is typically resolved without shipping defects.
rows.sat_rat: Allocation thresholds are tracked but occasional undersizing against planned output still occurs. Allocation monitoring is real-time. Sizing allocation to run output requires planning judgement. Allocation exhaustion stops production at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a traceability device becoming inoperable after an interrupted firmware update
rows.plain: Make it less likely that a device used to track products or equipment stops working after a software update is interrupted. This helps preserve tracking information and device operation.
rows.imp_rat: A traceability device bricked by an interrupted firmware update creates a hard gap in line traceability, so relevance to keeping the traceability job running is moderately high; however, severity is tempered because such interruptions are relatively infrequent, often recoverable via re-flash or device swap, and can be mitigated with rollback logic. Capped per the batch-2 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Modern devices increasingly ship with dual-image/rollback firmware, so failures are moderately contained but not universal across older installed base. A bricked device after an interrupted update means extended downtime while it is reflashed or replaced. Recovery from a failed firmware update typically requires vendor support or specialist technicians. A bricked traceability device can require costly replacement hardware and emergency vendor callouts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of provisioned serials inaccessible to the assignment workflow when requested
rows.plain: Make sure the reserved identification numbers can be reached and used when the assignment process needs them. This prevents delays or missing identifiers.
rows.imp_rat: Provisioned serials inaccessible to the workflow when requested stalls assignment; moderately severe as it disrupts flow but is usually recoverable before shipment.
rows.sat_rat: Serial access is usually reliable, but integration or network hiccups occasionally make requested serials unavailable. Inaccessible serials cause a hard wait, so timeliness is directly impacted. Diagnosing access failures requires IT/integration skill. Blocked assignment idles the line, driving cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of available serials being exhausted during a production run
rows.plain: Make sure enough unique identification numbers are available for the entire production run. Running out could stop the line.
rows.imp_rat: Serials exhausted during a run stops assignment; moderately severe as it causes stoppages but is usually caught before non-compliant shipment.
rows.sat_rat: Buffered pools and alerts make mid-run exhaustion uncommon but not eliminated when top-up is slow. Serial availability is monitored live during the run. Requesting timely top-ups needs operator awareness. Running out mid-run causes expensive downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exhausting available serials before the production run is complete
rows.plain: Make sure the supply of unique identification numbers does not run out before production is finished. Otherwise, labeling may have to stop.
rows.imp_rat: Exhausting serials before run completion halts assignment; moderately severe as it stops the run but rarely ships defective product directly.
rows.sat_rat: This near-duplicate depletion concern is served by the same mature threshold-alert practice with occasional gaps. Depletion tracking is continuous. Minimal skill needed once threshold alerts are configured. Exhaustion before completion incurs costly line stops.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of planned run demand not covered by valid serial identifiers
rows.plain: Make sure the planned work has enough valid identification numbers to cover it. This helps prevent the work from stopping because numbers are missing or unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Run demand not covered by valid serials threatens completion of assignment; moderately severe as it causes stoppages but is typically caught before shipment.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-run pool provisioning against planned quantities is standard, but demand changes and overage allowances still leave occasional coverage gaps mid-run. Provisioning shortfalls can halt a run, so this can directly cost run time when pools run dry. Estimating demand and requesting adequate pools requires planning judgement that is often manual. Under-provisioning triggers costly stoppages while over-provisioning wastes ranges, so balancing has real cost implications.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of physically present child units absent from the parent record at validation
rows.plain: Make sure every physical smaller unit is included in the record for the larger unit during the check. This prevents missing equipment from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Physically present child units absent from the parent aggregation record breaks the parent-child genealogy that is central to traceability (relevance); consequences include shipment holds and mis-aggregation but are often catchable at validation (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Count-based aggregation verification catches many discrepancies, but hand-packing and partial cases regularly leave physically present children off the parent record. Parent-child validation at packing is quick when counts match but stalls when they don't. Resolving missing child units at validation depends on operator diligence and manual verification. Missing children force re-scanning or opening cases, adding recurring labor and delay cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of authorized serial identifiers becoming unavailable before batch completion
rows.plain: Make sure the approved tracking numbers do not run out or become unavailable before the batch is finished. This allows every item to keep its correct identity.
rows.imp_rat: Authorized serials becoming unavailable before batch completion halts assignment; moderately severe as it stops the line but is usually recoverable without shipping defects.
rows.sat_rat: Provisioning large serial pools before batch start makes exhaustion uncommon, though connectivity to central systems can still interrupt supply. Pre-loaded serial pools mean availability rarely delays the run. Ordering adequate serials ahead requires planning but little in-line skill. Running short mid-batch can waste line time and materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serialization record changes lacking an attributable audit trail
rows.plain: Record who made each change to product-identification records, along with when and what was changed. This makes the change history trustworthy and reviewable.
rows.imp_rat: Record changes lacking an attributable audit trail is a data-integrity governance gap; severe because missing attribution can invalidate records during audit, though not a direct product defect.
rows.sat_rat: Part -style audit trails are mature and comprehensive, so changes lacking an attributable trail are very uncommon in validated systems. Audit-trail capture is automatic and imposes no added time. No operator skill is needed since trails are system-generated. Audit logging is a built-in feature with negligible incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of intermittent package-identifier read failures remaining undetected during verification
rows.plain: Make sure occasional failures to read package labels are found during checking. This prevents packages from being passed as acceptable when their labels were missed.
rows.imp_rat: Intermittent identifier read failures remaining undetected during verification let unreadable units pass (relevance), risking downstream traceability gaps (severity), though verification is a check stage rather than production.
rows.sat_rat: Verification stations catch outright failures well but intermittent, marginal reads slip through standard checks fairly often. Intermittent misreads are caught only when someone reviews reject logs, adding investigation delay. Distinguishing intermittent read failures from genuine no-reads requires analytical skill many line operators do not have. Undetected intermittent failures create suspect product batches that are expensive to reconcile or scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required package relationships and events missing from the returned genealogy
rows.plain: Make sure the returned product history includes all required links between packages and all important events involving them. This helps people trace what happened to each package.
rows.imp_rat: Missing package relationships or events in the returned genealogy defeats the purpose of a traceability query (relevance) and can block recall investigations, though queries can be re-run (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Genealogy returns are only as complete as upstream capture, so aggregation and event gaps propagate into missing relationships in the returned tree. Genealogy is generated on query, so retrieval is fast once records exist. Interpreting incomplete genealogy and filling gaps requires quality-system expertise. Incomplete genealogy on recall drives expensive over-broad containment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected packages with duplicate identifiers left undiscovered during exception handling
rows.plain: Reduce the number of packages with repeated identification numbers that go unnoticed while the problem is being handled. This helps ensure each package can be tracked correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate identifiers left undiscovered during exception handling propagate ambiguous serial data (relevance) that can corrupt genealogy and trigger recalls (severity), though exceptions are a bounded subset of volume.
rows.sat_rat: Exception handling frequently spawns duplicate identifiers that current tools do not reliably surface, leaving a persistent pool of undiscovered duplicates. Discovering hidden duplicates during exception handling is a slow, manual investigative task. Spotting duplicates created during exceptions relies on skilled analysts scanning logs after the fact. Undiscovered duplicates cause costly downstream errors and rework when they later surface.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that declining code-read success remains undetected until traceability integrity is affected
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that a decline in the system's ability to read labels or codes goes unnoticed until product tracking is affected. This helps keep the right products linked to the right information.
rows.imp_rat: Declining code-read success remaining undetected until integrity is affected is a monitoring lag (relevance) that permits accumulating data loss before intervention (severity), but it is a warning-layer rather than capture error.
rows.sat_rat: While no-read counts are logged, proactive trend alerting on declining read success is inconsistently deployed, so degradation often goes unnoticed until integrity is compromised. Read-rate degradation is often noticed late, after quality is already affected, delaying response. Detecting a slow decline requires an attentive engineer watching trends rather than a simple alarm. Undetected drift can spoil large volumes before it is caught, driving significant scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of claimed regulatory capabilities lacking verifiable compliance evidence
rows.plain: Make sure more of the claimed legal and safety requirements have clear proof that they are being met. This makes compliance claims trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Claimed regulatory capabilities lacking verifiable evidence is a compliance-assurance error (relevance) that can lead to audit findings or market blocks (severity), though it surfaces at qualification rather than runtime.
rows.sat_rat: Buyers increasingly demand documentation packages, but many claimed capabilities still lack traceable evidence, so the failure recurs. Requesting and reviewing compliance evidence adds review cycles that vendors sometimes delay. Distinguishing genuine verifiable compliance evidence from marketing claims requires regulatory affairs expertise not always at the table. Unverified claims lead to failed audits and remediation costs downstream rather than acquisition-phase spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serials commissioned before the corresponding label code is printed and verified
rows.plain: Do not activate product identification numbers until the matching label has been printed and checked. This helps prevent products from being linked to the wrong labels.
rows.imp_rat: Serials commissioned before their code is printed/verified is a sequencing error; moderately severe because it can create prematurely active serials but is often reconcilable.
rows.sat_rat: Commission-after-verify sequencing is configurable and generally well controlled in mature lines. Ordering of commission vs print is enforced inline in real time. Sequence enforcement is automated, reducing operator dependence. Premature commissioning risks reconciliation waste of little material value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identified packages passing line stations without position or status updates
rows.plain: Make sure every identified package receives an update about its position and current status as it moves through each station. This makes it possible to know where each package is and what has happened to it.
rows.imp_rat: Identified packages passing stations without position/status updates creates gaps in the movement trace that is central to the job (relevance) and can invalidate aggregation, though single missed stations may be recoverable (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Encoder-based tracking and station sensors reliably update most units, but sensor misses and accumulation zones leave some units passing without updates. Station position/status updates are logged in real time without adding delay. Sensor and PLC integration for station tracking is set up once and needs little operator skill. Missing position updates cause costly tracking loss and downstream reconciliation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected configuration cannot support required traceability records and data-integrity controls
rows.plain: Choose a system setup that can record who did what and preserve the accuracy of those records. This makes the information trustworthy and reviewable.
rows.imp_rat: A configuration unable to support required records and integrity controls is a selection-stage risk (moderate relevance) that, if it materializes, forces costly re-architecture and compliance gaps (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established data-integrity checklists and vendor audits reduce mismatch risk, but configuration nuances still slip through often enough to remain a real recurring gap. Verifying data-integrity and record-support capabilities lengthens vendor evaluation but is generally scheduled into the qualification timeline. Judging whether a configuration truly supports ALCOA+ records and audit trails requires deep GMP data-integrity expertise that few buying teams possess in-house. A wrong choice forces expensive re-validation or replacement, though the assessment cost during acquisition is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the candidate system cannot sustain required throughput under the expected product mix
rows.plain: Make sure the proposed system can keep up with the required production speed for the expected variety of products.
rows.imp_rat: A system unable to sustain throughput under the expected mix threatens line uptime and data capture (relevance); consequences include line slowdowns and missed scans (severity), but this is a capacity-selection risk not a core error.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor benchmarks and reference lines give reasonable confidence, yet mix variability and peak conditions are frequently under-tested, leaving meaningful residual risk. Throughput validation against product mix can be modeled quickly with vendor performance data during selection. Assessing sustained throughput under real product mix needs line-engineering judgement that is available but not uniformly applied. Under-sizing is caught after installation and is very costly to remedy, while the pre-purchase analysis is relatively cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of activating a production context that does not match the scheduled run
rows.plain: Prevent the plant from starting with the wrong set of production details. This helps ensure that the planned product is made using the correct instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Activating a production context not matching the scheduled run can mislabel or misattribute traceability data (relevance) with potential batch mix-ups (severity), though it is an operator-setup error that is often caught early.
rows.sat_rat: Modern MES-integrated traceability systems increasingly enforce scheduled-job binding and scan-to-confirm work orders, but manual override paths and standalone lines still let context mismatches slip through. Line changeover and job-selection steps take modest time today, and operators can usually verify the active context quickly against the scheduler. Selecting the right production context still leans on operator attention and familiarity with the MES/scheduler screen, so distracted or newer operators occasionally mispick. A mismatched context wastes materials and can force costly rework or line stoppage, though such events are relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required traceability records missing from the long-term archive
rows.plain: Keep all required records showing what happened during the work in the long-term storage system. This helps people prove what was done and review it later.
rows.imp_rat: Required records missing from the long-term archive undermines retention obligations central to traceability (relevance) and can cause regulatory non-compliance during retrospective audits (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated data-migration and retention practices under GxP/ Part most gaps, yet silent omissions during export from legacy systems still occur and are hard to fully prove closed. Exporting and migrating archives at retirement takes weeks but is a planned activity with predictable timelines. Records managers and validation staff know regulatory retention rules, though verifying completeness of a full archive still demands careful manual reconciliation. Long-term archival storage carries ongoing cost but is a budgeted, accepted expense in regulated environments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required functional, technical, regulatory, and integration requirements omitted from the specification
rows.plain: The written system plan should leave out as few important needs as possible, including practical, technical, legal, and connection needs. A complete plan helps ensure the finished system does what is required.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted requirements from the specification is an upstream planning error that indirectly affects the traceability job (moderate relevance) and can cause rework or gaps, though it is typically discoverable before deployment (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Template-driven URS practices catch many items yet integration and regulatory requirements are still routinely omitted, making incomplete specs a recurring failure. Building a complete requirements specification takes weeks of cross-functional workshops that rarely close every gap before the RFP deadline. Capturing functional, technical, regulatory, and integration requirements demands scarce senior engineers, QA, and IT experts whose absence leaves whole categories unstated. Omissions surface as costly change orders and rework after contract award, but the specification effort itself is not the main money sink.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of captured unit events missing required scanner, rejection, or timestamp fields
rows.plain: Make sure every recorded production event includes the required scanner information, rejection information, and time. Complete records make it possible to understand what happened during production.
rows.imp_rat: Captured events missing scanner, rejection, or timestamp fields weakens record completeness central to audits (relevance), but individual missing fields are often reconstructable and non-catastrophic (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated timestamping and device tagging populate required fields consistently, though occasional device/clock faults still leave some fields missing. Required fields are stamped automatically at capture with no added time. Field schemas are defined during commissioning, requiring little in-run attention. Incomplete event fields force costly data cleanup before records are usable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of defective or unreadable traceability codes accepted after calibration
rows.plain: Prevent defective or unreadable tracking codes from being accepted after the camera-based inspection system is adjusted. This helps ensure products can be identified and followed through the process.
rows.imp_rat: Defective or unreadable codes accepted after calibration lets bad codes into the stream (relevance) risking later read failures, but calibration is periodic and effects are often downstream-detectable (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Code-grading standards exist, but marginal codes accepted after calibration remain a recurring quality gap. Vision/code calibration and verification runs add setup time but are standard procedure. Proper calibration and grading interpretation requires trained vision-system technicians. Accepting bad codes causes downstream waste, partly offset by grading checks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning an incorrect identity to a unit during manual reconciliation
rows.plain: Avoid matching a unit to the wrong identity when its identifying code is missing. Correct matching keeps records and products connected to the right unit.
rows.imp_rat: Assigning incorrect identity during manual reconciliation defeats the core purpose of tracing units (relevance), and a mislabeled unit can propagate wrong recall/pedigree data (severity), though ranked below prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Manual reconciliation remains an ad hoc, error-prone workaround with few automated guardrails, so wrong-identity assignments recur whenever automated capture fails. Manual reconciliation is slow, requiring operators to stop and match units to records, and errors surface late. Correctly assigning identity by hand depends heavily on operator judgement and vigilance, which is exactly where the error concentrates and where standard practice offers little support. Misidentified units drive scrap, recall exposure, and investigation labour that the plant absorbs directly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of traceability exception scenarios lacking a defined handling path
rows.plain: Provide a clear way to handle every unusual situation that can make product records incomplete or inconsistent. This helps workers know what to do when normal tracking does not work.
rows.imp_rat: Undefined handling paths for exception scenarios is a process-completeness gap (moderate-low relevance); when hit, exceptions go unmanaged causing data gaps (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Exception coverage is chronically incomplete because happy-path configuration is prioritized, leaving many scenarios unhandled. Enumerating and defining every exception-handling path is a laborious, often shortchanged prep task. Anticipating exception scenarios requires deep operational experience that is scarce. Undefined handling paths cause costly line stops and manual firefighting later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of line stoppages caused by serial identifiers becoming unavailable for assignment
rows.plain: Shorten production stoppages caused by the system running out of numbers used to identify products. This helps work continue without losing track of items.
rows.imp_rat: Line stoppages from unavailable serials is a throughput/delay issue peripheral to the compliance CFJ; low severity because it delays rather than ships defects.
rows.sat_rat: Buffered local serial pools make such stoppages infrequent but not eliminated when upstream systems fail. When serials do run out, restoring supply causes a measurable line stoppage. Recovery often needs IT or supervisor intervention rather than the operator alone. Stoppage time is lost productive capacity and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of readable identifiers classified as unreadable during production
rows.plain: Mistakenly label fewer readable product identifiers as unreadable during production. This helps avoid unnecessary checks, delays, or rejects.
rows.imp_rat: Readable identifiers classified as unreadable is a false-reject verification error; low severity because it causes scrap and delay rather than shipping bad codes.
rows.sat_rat: False-unreadable classification persists as a tuning-dependent nuisance at line speed. Over-classification as unreadable causes reject-review time. Reducing false unreadables needs vision-tuning expertise. Readable units scrapped as unreadable waste product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to make assigned serial identifiers available for printing
rows.plain: Reduce the time needed to provide the assigned identification numbers so they can be printed. This helps the production line keep moving without waiting for labels.
rows.imp_rat: Time to make serials available for printing is a throughput/latency concern peripheral to compliance; low severity because it delays rather than causes defective output.
rows.sat_rat: Local buffering reliably keeps serials ready, with rare exceptions when provisioning fails. Pre-provisioned and cached serial pools make codes available for printing almost instantly. No operator skill is needed once pools are loaded. Any delay here idles the line, but caching largely avoids that cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of valid serial codes rejected at production line speed
rows.plain: Reject fewer valid codes when checking them at normal production speed. This helps the line keep moving without mistakenly stopping or rejecting good products.
rows.imp_rat: Valid codes rejected at line speed is a false-reject yield/throughput issue peripheral to compliance; low severity because good product is delayed, not shipped bad.
rows.sat_rat: False-reject rates on valid codes remain a persistent nuisance at line speed, tied to lighting and focus variability. False rejects trigger stoppages and manual review that cost line time. Tuning camera thresholds to avoid over-rejection requires vision-system expertise. Good units wrongly rejected are wasted product and packaging.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serialized labels rejected because their printed codes are unreadable
rows.plain: Reject fewer labels because their printed codes cannot be read. This helps keep correctly printed labels on products and reduces production delays.
rows.imp_rat: Labels rejected because printed codes are unreadable is largely a scrap/yield outcome; low severity since the reject correctly prevents bad product from advancing.
rows.sat_rat: Grade-checking cameras catch unreadable codes, but print-quality drift still generates ongoing reject rates. Unreadable rejects are caught inline without slowing the line materially. Print-quality tuning requires operator skill to set up correctly. Rejected labels consume serials and materials, a recurring cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of valid codes incorrectly rejected during verification
rows.plain: Reject fewer correct codes by mistake during checking. This helps prevent good products from being held back or rejected unnecessarily.
rows.imp_rat: Valid codes incorrectly rejected during verification is a false-reject yield concern peripheral to compliance; low severity because good product is scrapped, not shipped bad.
rows.sat_rat: Modern grading reduces false rejects, but threshold sensitivity still causes conforming units to be rejected periodically. False-reject decisions are made inline without delay. Tuning grading thresholds to avoid false rejects requires expertise. False rejects scrap good units and serials, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of conforming units rejected during code verification
rows.plain: Reject fewer correctly marked units when checking their printed codes. This helps prevent good products from being mistakenly rejected.
rows.imp_rat: Conforming units rejected during verification is a false-reject/yield loss peripheral to the compliance job; low severity as it protects rather than harms downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Vision verification is mature but over-rejection of good units remains a recurring line-yield concern. Verification disposition is immediate. Minimizing good-unit rejection needs skilled camera calibration. Rejecting conforming units wastes product and serials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that deployed traceability services fail to start in the designated environment
rows.imp_rat: Services failing to start in the designated environment blocks traceability capture (moderate relevance) but is usually caught at deployment before production impact (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Environment validation and deployment scripts make startup failures infrequent, though environment drift still bites occasionally. Deploying and smoke-testing services in the target environment is a defined, moderate-duration step. It requires IT/deployment expertise to align services with the runtime environment. Failed startups cause schedule cost but are usually caught before go-live.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of traceability components omitted from the synchronization scope
rows.imp_rat: Traceability components omitted from synchronization scope is a scoping/setup gap (lower relevance) that can leave data unsynced but is typically identified and corrected during configuration (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scope definition is largely manual and checklist-driven, so components are routinely missed from synchronization scope. Defining full synchronization scope is a detailed, time-intensive scoping exercise. Knowing every component that must sync requires broad system-architecture knowledge that is easy to under-specify. Omitted components cause expensive gaps found only in later testing.

rows.source_job: Configure rejection thresholds and station behavior
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of nonconforming units classified as acceptable by configured reject thresholds
rows.imp_rat: Classifying nonconforming units as acceptable is more a quality-inspection function than pure traceability (moderate relevance), but shipping defective product carries serious safety consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Threshold configuration is often under-validated at prep, so nonconforming units classified as acceptable is a real, persistent risk. Tuning reject thresholds requires iterative test runs that consume prep time. Setting correct thresholds demands nuanced judgement about false-accept vs false-reject trade-offs. Nonconformers passed as acceptable create costly escapes and complaints.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of logical station connections not matching physical product flow
rows.imp_rat: Logical station connections not matching physical flow is a configuration-mapping error (lower relevance) that can misroute status updates, though it is usually caught during commissioning (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Physical-flow walkdowns and simulation catch most mismatches, though logical/physical drift still appears during startup. Mapping logical stations to physical flow is a defined commissioning step of moderate duration. Correct mapping relies on line-engineer familiarity with the actual product path. Mismatches force re-commissioning, a contained but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of live production traceability records incorrectly altered during a training run
rows.imp_rat: Altering live production records during a training run corrupts authentic traceability data (moderate relevance), with meaningful integrity/audit consequences (severity), yet ranked lower here.
rows.sat_rat: Only better systems provide isolated training/sandbox environments, so where they are absent training runs routinely risk altering production records with no hard barrier. Switching into and out of a training mode is quick when a dedicated mode exists, but reverting improperly can leave live records exposed. Whether training touches live data depends on operators knowing to use a sandbox or non-production environment, which many systems do not clearly separate. Corrupted live traceability records can trigger expensive data-integrity investigations and remediation under GMP scrutiny.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of linking an exception annotation to the wrong batch, lot, or packaging event
rows.imp_rat: Linking an exception annotation to the wrong batch/lot corrupts the traceability narrative (moderate relevance) and can misdirect investigations (moderate severity), ranked below prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Context-aware systems that auto-associate annotations to the active batch help substantially, but manual entry and shared screens still allow wrong-batch linkage. Annotating an exception against a batch is a brief step, and time is rarely the limiting factor. Choosing the correct batch/lot context for an annotation relies on the operator understanding which event is active, a modest but real vigilance demand. A misattributed annotation can widen or misdirect a recall or hold, inflating cost when the error escapes review.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that vision readers remain outside tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: Vision readers out of tolerance after calibration threatens accurate code reading central to tracing (moderate relevance), producing misreads (moderate severity), held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Post-calibration verification checks are standard, so readers landing outside tolerance undetected is fairly uncommon though not rare. Calibration procedures are well-defined and complete within a maintenance window. Vision calibration follows documented routines but still relies on a trained technician to judge borderline results. Recalibration uses standard targets with modest consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of traceability records or configuration settings corrupted or lost during the update
rows.imp_rat: Corruption or loss of traceability records/config during updates directly undermines the chain of custody central to the job (relevance high), and lost records can break regulatory traceability (severity moderate-high), but capped by ranking to 4.5.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-update backups are standard practice, so most corruption is recoverable though verification of completeness remains imperfect. Recovering corrupted records or settings after a bad update consumes significant restore and re-entry time. Verifying record integrity after an update requires database and validation knowledge beyond routine maintenance staff. Corrupted serialization data can force product holds with direct financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required traceability-system configuration elements omitted from the backup
rows.imp_rat: Omitting config elements from backup is a recovery-completeness concern (moderate-low relevance) that slows or complicates restoration (moderate severity) rather than losing traceability data itself.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration capture is common but frequently partial because undocumented tweaks and dependencies get omitted. Configuration backup is quick and often bundled with routine data backups. Knowing every config element that must be captured requires system-specific expertise that varies widely. Missing config elements mean costly manual rebuild time during recovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restored traceability records that are incomplete or inconsistent
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete or inconsistent restored records defeats the point of having backups for traceability (moderate relevance) and can leave gaps in the audit trail (severity), held under 4.5.
rows.sat_rat: Restores are performed infrequently and seldom drill-tested, so incompleteness surfaces unpredictably at the worst moment. Discovering incomplete restores mid-recovery forces slow re-attempts and reconciliation. Validating restored data consistency demands database expertise rarely present on the maintenance floor. Inconsistent restored records can invalidate batch release and trigger costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required serialization, aggregation, and event records omitted from the backup
rows.imp_rat: Omitting serialization/aggregation/event records from backup weakens recovery of traceability data (moderate relevance) and could mean irrecoverable loss during failure (severity), capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backup jobs are routine but scope-completeness across serialization, aggregation, and event tables is often untested until a real restore exposes gaps. Automated scheduled backups run without added operator time in most modern deployments. Confirming that all record types are actually captured requires knowledge of the data schema that few maintainers verify. Missed records in backup risk expensive regulatory non-compliance if a restore is ever needed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of historical traceability data lost or corrupted during archival
rows.imp_rat: Loss/corruption during archival affects historical data less central to live line operation (lower relevance) but permanent loss of regulated history is a serious compliance issue (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Archival with checksums exists in mature systems but long-term media/format integrity is rarely re-verified, leaving latent corruption. Archival runs are typically automated background processes requiring little active time. Verifying archive integrity requires storage and data-management skills not routinely applied. Lost historical data can expose the site to compliance penalties years later.

rows.source_job: Transfer system assets to designated recipient
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transferred hardware retains regulated production or confidential data
rows.imp_rat: Transferred hardware retaining regulated/confidential data is peripheral to the traceability job itself (low relevance) but a data-breach exposure carries high severity when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Standard IT asset-disposal practice covers obvious drives well, but confidential production data hiding in firmware, controllers or forgotten peripherals routinely slips through, leaving real residual risk. Wiping or physically destroying drives before hardware transfer is a bounded task but adds scheduling delay to disposal. Reliable data sanitization requires IT security judgement to identify every storage location including embedded and cached copies, which floor-level disposal crews often lack. Certified wiping tools and destruction services cost money but are far cheaper than a breach.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical traceability functions failing qualification tests
rows.imp_rat: Critical traceability functions failing qualification tests is a pre-deployment gate error (lower relevance) that, while serious if released, is normally caught by qualification itself (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Formal IQ/OQ/PQ practice is mature and systematically surfaces failing functions, keeping this reasonably served. Qualification testing of traceability functions is a lengthy, formally scheduled prep activity. Executing and interpreting qualification protocols demands validation-engineering expertise. Test failures late in prep are costly to remediate, motivating structured protocols.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of captured serialization records omitted from the production-event report
rows.imp_rat: Omitting captured records from a production-event report is a reporting-completeness gap (moderate-low relevance) with modest severity since the underlying data still exists.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization aggregation and reconciliation logic in mature L2/L3 systems reliably rolls captured reads into reports, though buffer losses and comms dropouts still cause occasional omissions. Report generation is largely automated and fast once records are captured. Little operator skill is needed since aggregation into the production-event report is machine-driven. Omitted serialization records can force reconciliation labour or delay batch release, but such gaps are usually caught downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed maintenance, calibration, or validation events omitted from the system history
rows.imp_rat: Omitting maintenance/calibration/validation events from system history is an audit-record completeness gap (moderate-low relevance) with moderate consequences for equipment-qualification evidence.
rows.sat_rat: Manual logging of maintenance and calibration events is inconsistently done, so omissions from system history are common. Logging events after the fact adds little time when done, but catching omissions requires slow audits. Complete event capture depends on individual discipline rather than skill. Missing maintenance history undermines audit readiness with compliance cost exposure.

rows.source_job: Purge sensitive data from retired system
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of recoverable sensitive data remaining on retired storage devices after purging
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable sensitive data on retired devices is largely peripheral to the traceability CFJ (low relevance) but a residual-data breach carries notable severity when exploited.
rows.sat_rat: Physical destruction with certificates is highly dependable, though purge-only (non-destructive) methods on modern SSDs occasionally leave recoverable remnants that go unverified. Cryptographic erase and degaussing are quick, and physical shredding is fast when outsourced. NIST - procedures are well documented, reducing the expertise needed to purge to a known standard. Certified destruction adds per-device cost but is routine and modest for a disposal budget.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of references not representing comparable sterile fill-finish environments
rows.imp_rat: The error of using references that don't represent comparable sterile fill-finish environments is only moderately relevant to packaging line traceability's core job of tracking and identifying units through the line—it concerns benchmark/reference selection rather than the direct trace function. Severity is contained since a poor comparison reference degrades analytical quality but does not itself break traceability or cause irreversible product/compliance failure, consistent with this batch's lower-ranked importance ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors supply favorable but non-comparable references and few buyers rigorously filter them, so this is a chronic weak spot. Sourcing and contacting comparable references takes effort that is often abbreviated. Judging whether a reference truly matches a sterile fill-finish environment requires domain knowledge many evaluators lack. Poor references cause a bad selection whose costs appear only after deployment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that negotiated data-integrity and validation responsibilities conflict with sterile fill-finish compliance obligations
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting negotiated integrity responsibilities with sterile fill-finish obligations is a contractual/governance edge concern (low relevance) but compliance conflicts can be serious when they surface (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no mature standard practice for locking these responsibility boundaries, so conflicts surface late and often, keeping this poorly served. Reconciling negotiated responsibilities against sterile fill-finish obligations extends contract negotiation. Aligning data-integrity and validation responsibility splits with sterile GMP compliance requires rare combined QA-legal-regulatory judgement. Responsibility conflicts create disputed rework and liability costs that are hard to price during negotiation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required plant-floor and enterprise interfaces omitted from the integration assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitting interfaces from the integration assessment is a planning-completeness defect (moderate-low relevance) causing rework or gaps (moderate severity) that are usually correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Integration assessments routinely omit legacy or enterprise touchpoints, making omission a common and disruptive recurring failure. Mapping every plant-floor and enterprise interface is time-consuming and often truncated under schedule pressure. Complete interface inventories require OT/IT integration specialists who understand both MES/ERP and line-level systems. Missed interfaces trigger expensive integration change orders later rather than large upfront cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prior-batch serialization settings remaining active after changeover
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch settings left active after changeover risks wrong-market serials with moderate severity but secondary relevance; capped by ranking.
